primary_hypothesis: The West vendor staffing transition degraded incident handling during the observation window, causing West's higher aggregate incident rate independently of routed-item severity.
rival_hypothesis: The aggregate regional disparity is caused by different severity mixes: West received a much larger share of high-severity items, while West and East had the same incident rate within each recorded severity stratum.
predicted_discrimination: A zero or negligible maximum within-severity West/East rate gap would support the severity-mix rival and weigh against staffing-related degradation independent of severity. A substantial gap, especially one consistently favoring East, would support the primary hypothesis over severity mix alone, although it would not by itself prove that the staffing transition caused the gap.
bounded_conclusion: For the supplied observation-window records, the West/East aggregate incident-rate disparity is explained by the recorded severity composition: the regions' incident rates match within both listed severity strata, while West routed proportionally more high-severity items. These records do not establish vendor underperformance, staffing-transition causation, or grounds for sanction.
residual_uncertainty: The records do not measure possible confounders within the high- and low-severity strata, and they do not show whether the staffing transition affected which items were routed to West; finer severity or case-mix differences could therefore remain.
recommended_consequence: Abstain from vendor sanction on this record. Escalate only to a case-mix-adjusted review using finer within-stratum routing and incident data, including the staffing transition period, before attributing the disparity to vendor performance.
